evaluations.description: Critical path evaluations for AnalyzeQueryCostTool — covers cost estimation accuracy, optimization detection, risk gating, and input safety. These represent the highest-impact failure modes in production.

evaluation.name: Cost Estimation and Tier Classification
evaluation.description: Verify that the tool correctly estimates cost from dry-run bytes and assigns the right cost tier. This is the core contract of the tool — if this is wrong, nothing downstream can be trusted.
evaluation.scenario:
A data analyst submits a straightforward SELECT query against a large table:
SELECT user_id, event_type, created_at FROM `project.dataset.events` WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01'
The BigQuery dry-run returns 15GB bytes_processed (16106127360 bytes).
evaluation.expected_steps: 1. Call execute() with the SQL above 2. Tool performs dry-run via _perform_dry_run_analysis() 3. Tool calls _calculate_cost_estimates() with bytes_processed = 16106127360 4. Cost is calculated as: (15 / 1024) TB * $6.25 = ~$0.0916 5. Cost tier is classified as LOW (between $0 and $1) 6. Result is returned with success: true
evaluation.success_criteria: - estimated_cost_usd is within 1% of $0.0916 - cost_tier is "LOW" - bytes_to_process equals 16106127360 - price_per_tb in cost_breakdown equals 6.25 - success is true

evaluation.name: Optimization Pattern Detection — Multiple Violations
evaluation.description: Verify the tool correctly identifies all optimization anti-patterns present in a poorly written query. Missing even one pattern means real money is left on the table in production.
evaluation.scenario:
A new engineer submits this query for cost review:
SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.large_events` ORDER BY created_at
This query has three distinct anti-patterns: SELECT *, no WHERE clause, and ORDER BY without LIMIT.
evaluation.expected_steps: 1. Call execute() with include_optimization=true and the SQL above 2. Tool calls _generate_optimization_suggestions() with pattern-based model 3. Pattern matcher checks for: select_star, no_where_clause, no_limit_with_order 4. All three patterns are matched against the SQL 5. Three distinct suggestions are returned
evaluation.success_criteria: - optimization_suggestions contains exactly 3 items (no duplicates) - One suggestion references replacing SELECT * with specific columns - One suggestion references adding a WHERE clause - One suggestion references adding a LIMIT clause - include_optimization=false returns an empty optimization_suggestions list

evaluation.name: Critical Risk Gate — High Cost Query Should Block Execution
evaluation.description: Verify that a query processing over 1TB is correctly flagged as CRITICAL risk and the execution_recommendation tells the caller not to run it. This is the primary safety mechanism of the tool — a wrong risk level could cause a five-figure BigQuery bill.
evaluation.scenario:
A pipeline engineer submits a full-scan query against a multi-TB table:
SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.raw_clickstream`
The dry-run returns 2199023255552 bytes (2TB). There is no WHERE clause, no partition filter, and it uses SELECT *.
evaluation.expected_steps: 1. Call execute() with the SQL above 2. _calculate_cost_estimates() produces ~$13.75 (2TB * $6.25 / 1024... wait — 2TB = 2 * $6.25 = $12.50), cost_tier = HIGH 3. _assess_execution_risk() accumulates risk_score from: - High cost (>$5): +2 - Extremely large data volume (>1TB): +4 - SELECT * on large dataset (>1GB): +2 - Full table scan without WHERE: +3 Total risk_score >= 8 → risk_level = CRITICAL 4. execution_recommendation reflects CRITICAL level
evaluation.success_criteria: - risk_assessment.risk_level is "CRITICAL" - risk_assessment.risk_score is >= 8 - risk_assessment.execution_recommendation contains "DO NOT EXECUTE" - risk_assessment.risk_factors includes entries for data volume and full table scan - risk_assessment.mitigation_steps is non-empty - cost_tier is "HIGH" or "CRITICAL"

evaluation.name: Input Validation — Malformed and Dangerous SQL
evaluation.description: Verify the tool rejects invalid inputs before making any network calls. Failing to validate early means wasting dry-run quota, risking BQ errors surfacing as unhandled exceptions, and exposing potential injection surfaces.
evaluation.scenario: Three separate calls are made to execute(): Case A — empty string: sql="" Case B — whitespace only: sql=" " Case C — DML statement: sql="DROP TABLE `project.dataset.events`"
evaluation.expected_steps: 1. Case A: execute() called with empty string - Tool detects empty SQL before any I/O - Returns success: false with a validation error message - No BigQuery API call is made 2. Case B: execute() called with whitespace-only string - Same early rejection path as Case A - No BigQuery API call is made 3. Case C: execute() called with DROP TABLE statement - _is_dml_query() (or equivalent validation) detects DML - Returns success: false with a clear rejection reason - No BigQuery API call is made
evaluation.success_criteria: - All three cases return success: false - No cases trigger a BigQuery dry-run API call - Error messages are human-readable and do not expose internal stack traces - Case C error message distinguishes DML rejection from empty-input rejection - Response time for all three is under 50ms (pure in-process validation)
